This skill heavily depends on an external MCP server (DeepWiki) with AI-generated responses, making it fundamentally unsuitable for lightweight mocking. The non-deterministic AI outputs and external HTTP dependency would produce brittle, unrealistic synthetic data that cannot be reliably simulated without the actual service.

This skill depends heavily on external Google API calls, real web searches, and a live GEMINI_API_KEY. It cannot be meaningfully mocked without actual external infrastructure, produces non-deterministic results based on real-time web content, and involves long-running jobs that are unsuitable for lightweight synthetic data generation.

This skill heavily depends on live external HTTP APIs (arXiv and Semantic Scholar) with real API keys, making it fundamentally unsuitable for lightweight mocking. The outputs are non-deterministic as academic databases change over time, and the pipeline would require simulating two complex external APIs with authentication.

This skill heavily depends on external HTTP calls to Plutio's live API with real authentication (API key/secret). It cannot be meaningfully mocked without building a complete Plutio API simulator, which defeats the lightweight pipeline goal. The v1.11 API also has significant limitations that would reduce data yield quality.

This skill is highly suitable for synthetic data generation - it operates on local files with clear, simple tool boundaries (memory_add, memory_grep, memory_summarize), has no external dependencies, and naturally supports multi-turn interactions around building and querying a personal knowledge base.

This skill wraps a well-documented public transport API (Navitia) with clear tool boundaries (places, journeys, incidents). While it requires an external API key, the API structure is stable and mockable. The skill supports realistic multi-turn scenarios (place resolution ambiguity, journey details, follow-up disruption checks) and has low state complexity. The main challenge is non-deterministic real-time data, but this can be addressed through controlled mock responses.

This skill heavily depends on external HTTP scraping of finviz.com and browser automation (Crawl4AI/Playwright), making it fundamentally unsuitable for lightweight mocking. The crawler requires live web requests, rate limiting, and real-time content extraction which cannot be reliably simulated. While the query tool is partially mockable, the core skill architecture is built around continuous external service dependency.

This skill requires mocking SMB file system operations, network mounts, and external Synology NAS hardware which are difficult to simulate faithfully in a lightweight pipeline. The heavy dependency on real network file operations and external hardware makes it unsuitable for stable mock-based data generation.

This skill requires real system access (file system, disk I/O, installed packages) and cannot be meaningfully mocked. It deletes files which is safety-sensitive, and its outputs are highly non-deterministic depending on the actual state of the system where it runs. The pipeline cannot simulate the required OS-level state.

This skill fundamentally depends on external HTTP requests to real carrier websites (USPS, UPS, FedEx, etc.) and browser-use automation, making it unsuitable for lightweight mocking. The core functionality (status checking) requires real external services that are non-deterministic, change frequently, and have bot detection. The browser-use fallback explicitly sends tracking data to cloud services, creating privacy and infrastructure dependencies that cannot be reliably simulated in a synthetic pipeline.

This skill depends on a live external API (Quodd) with real-time stock data that is inherently non-deterministic. Stock prices change constantly, making stable mocking difficult. It also requires authentication credentials, and the data is time-sensitive which would produce brittle synthetic trajectories.
